text: Block a consistent 7-8 hour sleep window by working backward from your required wake-up time.
notes: Rewritten from vague 'protect a realistic sleep opportunity window' to concrete action.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: You may keep pushing your bedtime later because evenings feel like your only free time. Decide on a wake-up time, count back 8 hours, and treat that as a boundary, not a suggestion.
personaContext.curious_fox: You might stay up exploring something interesting and tell yourself you'll catch up tomorrow. Set the window once and treat it as non-negotiable for one week before judging it.
personaContext.watchful_deer: Poor sleep makes everything feel harder the next day, and that feeling can spiral. A fixed window removes the nightly decision and takes the pressure off.
personaContext.steadfast_bear: You already push through fatigue, but that approach has a ceiling. A fixed sleep window isn't about being soft — it's about making tomorrow's performance predictable.
personaContext.steady_elephant: You do well with structure. Apply that same strength to sleep: a defined window, same time each night, becomes automatic within days.
personaContext.pack_wolf: If people around you stay up late, it's easy to match their schedule. Set your own window based on when you need to wake up, not when others go to bed.

text: Move your bedtime 15-30 minutes earlier for one week and notice how you feel.
notes: Small shift is easier to sustain than a dramatic change. Added 'notice how you feel' for self-awareness.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: You don't need to overhaul your entire schedule. Just 15 minutes earlier. That's small enough that it won't feel like a project.
personaContext.watchful_deer: A gradual shift means less pressure. You're not failing at a big change — you're testing a small one.

text: Do not sacrifice sleep for early morning workouts when you are under-recovered.
notes: Prevents training from worsening recovery debt.

personaContext.watchful_deer: Waking up exhausted to train can feel like discipline, but it often leads to a worse day and less motivation the next morning. Sleep is the workout your body needs first.
personaContext.steadfast_bear: Your instinct is to train regardless. But when recovery is poor, the early session costs more than it gains. Sleep first, train when your body has something to give.

text: Pick a wake-up time you can hit 5 out of 7 days and anchor your schedule around it.
notes: Rewritten from generic 'use a consistent schedule' to specific 5/7 anchor.

personaContext.steadfast_bear: You respond well to rules. A 5-out-of-7 anchor gives you enough structure without the guilt of missing the other two days.
personaContext.steady_elephant: Consistency is your strength. A fixed wake time is the single easiest anchor — everything else (bedtime, meals, workouts) can align to it.

text: Get 10 minutes of natural light within an hour of waking, especially if you work indoors.
notes: Rewritten with specificity: 10 minutes, within an hour, especially indoors workers.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: This doesn't require a morning routine overhaul. Step outside, stand near a window, or have your coffee in natural light. That's it.
personaContext.curious_fox: Morning light is one of the simplest evidence-based tools for sleep. If you like understanding why things work: it resets your circadian clock.
personaContext.watchful_deer: If mornings feel groggy or heavy, natural light helps your body understand that the day has started. It's gentle and requires no effort.
personaContext.steadfast_bear: Think of morning light as a performance tool. It sharpens your alertness curve and makes your evening wind-down more effective.
personaContext.steady_elephant: Add this to your morning sequence — it slots in easily alongside whatever you already do after waking.
personaContext.pack_wolf: If your mornings are social (coffee with family, walking with someone), do it near a window or outside. You get the benefit without changing the routine.

text: Avoid large weekend sleep schedule swings — sleeping in more than 90 minutes past your weekday wake time disrupts your rhythm.
notes: Added specific 90-minute threshold to make it actionable.

personaContext.steadfast_bear: You might use weekends to 'catch up' on sleep, but a 3-hour lie-in makes Monday morning feel like jet lag. Stay within 90 minutes of your weekday time.
personaContext.steady_elephant: Your weekday rhythm is an asset. Protect it on weekends — a small lie-in is fine, but a large swing resets the clock you've been building.

text: Use the same 20-30 minute wind-down sequence every night: dim lights, no screens, one calming activity.
notes: Added specific components (dim lights, no screens, one calming activity) to make it concrete.

personaContext.watchful_deer: Your mind tends to keep running after the day ends — replaying conversations, scanning for problems. This routine teaches your nervous system that the performance part of the day is over.
personaContext.steady_elephant: You do well with repeatable sequences. Build this the same way you build any habit: same order, same time, same place.

text: Tonight, pick one calming activity (reading, stretching, quiet music) and do it before bed for 7 nights in a row.
notes: Added concrete examples and 7-night commitment to make it a real first action.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: You don't need a full routine yet. One activity, repeated. That's it. The repetition is what makes it work, not the complexity.
personaContext.curious_fox: Resist the urge to try a different activity each night. The power here is in repetition — your brain starts associating this activity with sleep within a few days.

text: Avoid intense work, difficult conversations, or high-stimulation content in the hour before bed.
notes: Expanded from vague 'high-stimulation activity' to specific triggers.

personaContext.curious_fox: That late-night research rabbit hole feels productive, but it activates your brain right when it should be slowing down. Save it for tomorrow.
personaContext.watchful_deer: Difficult conversations or stressful emails before bed will replay in your head for hours. Set a cutoff time and mean it.

text: Charge your phone outside the bedroom, or across the room if you use it as an alarm.
notes: Merged SLP010 and SLP011 (both about bedtime scrolling). One environment change covers the behavior.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: If the phone is within reach, you'll scroll. It's not a willpower problem — it's a proximity problem. Move the phone and the habit breaks itself.
personaContext.watchful_deer: Late-night scrolling often starts as distraction and ends as anxiety. Removing the phone removes the trigger without requiring any discipline in the moment.

text: Dim the lights in your home 30-60 minutes before your target bedtime.
notes: Renumbered from SLP012. Simple environment change with strong evidence base.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: No special equipment needed — just turn off overhead lights and use a side lamp. The signal to your brain is immediate.
personaContext.curious_fox: If you're curious about the science: bright light suppresses melatonin production. Dimming lights is one of the easiest evidence-based sleep tools.
personaContext.watchful_deer: Bright environments keep your nervous system alert. Softer lighting helps your body transition from 'on' to 'winding down' without you having to force it.
personaContext.steadfast_bear: Think of it as setting the stage for recovery. Just as you'd set up equipment before a workout, dim lighting sets up your body for sleep.
personaContext.steady_elephant: Make this part of your evening sequence — at a set time, lights go down. It becomes automatic.
personaContext.pack_wolf: If you're with family in the evening, dimming shared spaces benefits everyone. It's a group-friendly change.

text: Keep your bedroom cool, dark, and quiet. Use blackout curtains, earplugs, or a fan if needed.
notes: Merged SLP013, SLP014, SLP031 into one comprehensive environment recommendation.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: You don't need to buy everything at once. Start with one change — a fan for noise, a curtain for light — and see what makes the biggest difference for you.
personaContext.curious_fox: Try different combinations — fans, curtains, earplugs — and settle on what works. Once you find it, stop experimenting and keep it consistent.
personaContext.watchful_deer: If noise or light wakes you, the disruption affects your mood the next day more than you might realize. Fix the environment first, before trying to fix the sleep.
personaContext.steadfast_bear: Your sleep environment is the foundation of your recovery system. Treat it like equipment maintenance — get the setup right once and it pays off every night.
personaContext.steady_elephant: Set up your bedroom once and leave it. Blackout curtains and a consistent temperature become part of your routine — no daily decisions required.
personaContext.pack_wolf: If you share a room, earplugs or a white noise machine can give you control over your sleep environment without requiring changes from others.

text: Avoid working from bed regularly — keep the bed associated with sleep, not productivity.
notes: Renumbered from SLP015. Clear rationale added.

personaContext.watchful_deer: If you associate your bed with deadlines and emails, your brain won't switch off when you lie down at night. Even a small desk or a specific chair creates the separation.
personaContext.steady_elephant: Structure matters to you. A physical boundary between work space and sleep space reinforces the mental boundary.

text: Stop caffeine at least 6-8 hours before your target bedtime. If you sleep at 11 PM, your last coffee should be by 3 PM.
notes: Merged SLP016 and SLP017. Added specific hour example to make it concrete.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: You don't need to quit coffee. Just set a cutoff time and stick to it for a week. The effect on sleep quality is often noticeable within days.
personaContext.curious_fox: Caffeine has a half-life of 5-6 hours. That means half the caffeine from a 4 PM coffee is still in your system at 10 PM. The math makes the rule easy to follow.
personaContext.watchful_deer: If your sleep is already fragile, afternoon caffeine can be the hidden factor. It doesn't stop you from falling asleep — it reduces sleep depth, so you wake up feeling unrested.
personaContext.steadfast_bear: You might rely on caffeine to push through fatigue. That works short-term but creates a cycle: caffeine → worse sleep → more fatigue → more caffeine. Break it at the timing level.
personaContext.steady_elephant: Set a specific cutoff time and treat it like a rule. It's one of the simplest high-impact sleep changes.
personaContext.pack_wolf: Social coffee runs or tea breaks are fine — just switch to decaf or water after your cutoff time. You still get the social benefit without the sleep cost.

text: If heavy meals disrupt your sleep, eat your last large meal at least 2-3 hours before bed.
notes: Merged SLP018 and SLP019. Made conditional since it only applies when meals affect sleep.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: If you notice heavy meals disrupting your sleep, this one simple rule removes the guesswork. No tracking needed — just a time gap.
personaContext.curious_fox: Experiment with the timing to find your window. Some people need 2 hours, others 3. Notice the difference and adjust.
personaContext.watchful_deer: If you track how you feel after late meals versus earlier ones, you will see the pattern clearly. Use the evidence to set your own cutoff.
personaContext.steadfast_bear: This is straightforward: eat your last big meal by a set time. Add it to your schedule and treat it as non-negotiable on training days.
personaContext.steady_elephant: Make this a fixed habit: last meal by a certain time, no exceptions. Once it becomes automatic, you stop thinking about it.
personaContext.pack_wolf: If you eat socially in the evenings, plan your portion size rather than skipping the meal. A lighter plate late is better than a full one.

text: On nights when you slept poorly, switch to walking, stretching, or a lighter session instead of forcing your full workout.
notes: Merged SLP020, SLP021, FIT016 into one clear recovery rule. Consolidated cross-pillar redundancy.

personaContext.watchful_deer: Forcing a hard session after bad sleep often leads to a worse day and a longer recovery. A lighter session protects both your body and your motivation.
personaContext.steadfast_bear: Your instinct is to push through. But training at 40% capacity while under-recovered often costs you the next day's session too. A light day protects the week.

text: Set a hard stop time for work screens, then do one non-work activity (walk, shower, reading) before bed.
notes: Rewritten from vague 'create a clear transition'. Now has specific action.

personaContext.watchful_deer: Without a hard stop, work bleeds into your evening and then into your sleep. The non-work activity is the buffer that separates your performance self from your resting self.
personaContext.steady_elephant: Treat the stop time like any other appointment. Once it's a rule, it stops requiring willpower.

text: If your mind races at bedtime, write tomorrow's top 3 tasks on paper before you lie down.
notes: Renumbered from SLP023. Added 'on paper' for specificity.

personaContext.watchful_deer: Your brain holds onto unfinished tasks. Writing them down gives your mind permission to let go — the list exists somewhere safe, so you don't have to keep rehearsing it.
personaContext.steady_elephant: This fits naturally into an evening routine. It takes 2 minutes and creates closure for the day.

text: One bad night does not erase a week of good sleep. Treat it as a blip, not a crisis.
notes: Rewritten from clinical 'do not catastrophize'. Now has proportional framing.

personaContext.watchful_deer: You tend to feel a bad night more intensely than it actually affects you. The anxiety about poor sleep often does more damage than the poor sleep itself. Tomorrow night will be different.

text: If tracking your sleep increases your anxiety about sleep, stop tracking and focus on how you feel instead.
notes: Renumbered from SLP025. Made more direct.

personaContext.watchful_deer: Some people check their sleep score and feel worse regardless of how they actually feel. If that's you, the tracker is working against you. Your subjective sense of rest matters more than a number.

text: During travel or disrupted weeks, keep one sleep anchor: same wake-up time OR same wind-down activity.
notes: Merged SLP026. Added OR choice for flexibility.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: When everything else changes, one constant is enough. Pick whichever anchor is easier to keep during travel.
personaContext.steadfast_bear: You want to maintain your full routine, but travel makes that impossible. One anchor keeps the thread alive without the frustration of trying to replicate home conditions.

text: If you work rotating shifts, do not force a rigid sleep schedule. Focus on sleep quality and duration when you can get it.
notes: Renumbered from SLP027.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: Shift work removes the predictability you rely on. Focus on what you can control: sleep environment and wind-down routine, regardless of the clock.
personaContext.curious_fox: Varying schedules actually suit your adaptability. Focus on sleep quality over timing — you can adjust faster than most.
personaContext.watchful_deer: Inconsistent schedules may feel chaotic. Focus on the elements you can stabilize: same wind-down routine, same environment, even if the hours shift.
personaContext.steadfast_bear: Your rigid schedule instinct will fight shift changes. Accept that fixed timing is not possible and redirect that discipline toward consistent sleep hygiene instead.
personaContext.steady_elephant: You prefer routine, so shifting sleep times feels wrong. Build your consistency around the ritual (same steps before bed) rather than the clock.
personaContext.pack_wolf: Coordinate with household members so they understand your shifted sleep needs. Social support around your schedule matters more for you than most.

text: Rest is part of the work. Muscles grow during recovery, not during training. Skipping rest is not discipline — it is sabotage.
notes: Rewritten from slogan-like 'treat recovery as productive, not lazy'. Now has specific framing.

personaContext.watchful_deer: If skipping a rest day feels lazy, that instinct is actually working against your goals. Recovery isn't the absence of effort — it's where the effort pays off.
personaContext.steadfast_bear: You respect hard work. Recovery is hard work done by your body while you step back. Fighting that process doesn't make you tougher — it makes the next session worse.

text: Do not stay up late reclaiming personal time you missed during the day. The trade-off is worse than it feels in the moment.
notes: NEW. Addresses revenge bedtime procrastination — a gap identified in v1.4 review.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: The evening feels like the only time that's truly yours. But borrowing it from sleep means tomorrow will feel even more drained, and you'll need even more recovery time tomorrow night. The cycle gets worse, not better.
personaContext.watchful_deer: After a demanding day, staying up feels like reclaiming control. But the next morning, the fatigue makes everything feel harder — including the things you stayed up to escape from.
personaContext.steady_elephant: If this is a pattern, it usually means your daytime schedule needs a boundary, not your nighttime. The real fix is protecting some free time during the day.

text: Use calming audio, a sleep app, or background sounds only if they help you unwind — not as another thing to optimize.
notes: Renumbered from SLP032. Added anti-optimization caveat.

personaContext.curious_fox: You might find yourself testing 10 different sleep sounds and rating them. Pick one that works and leave it. The point is relaxation, not research.
personaContext.watchful_deer: If background sounds quiet your mind, use them. If they become another source of 'am I doing this right?' anxiety, drop them.

text: If sleep problems persist for more than 4 weeks despite consistent behavior changes, seek professional support.
notes: Merged SLP029 and SLP030. Added 4-week timeline for specificity.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: Seeking professional help is not failure — it is the responsible next step when self-managed changes are not enough.
personaContext.curious_fox: You may keep trying new approaches instead of seeking help. If 4 weeks of changes have not worked, the next experiment is professional guidance.
personaContext.watchful_deer: You may delay seeking help because you want to solve it yourself. Persistent sleep issues are medical, not motivational — a professional is the right move.
personaContext.steadfast_bear: You may push through poor sleep as if discipline alone will fix it. After 4 weeks of consistent effort without improvement, discipline is not the issue.
personaContext.steady_elephant: If your reliable routine is not producing results after 4 weeks, the problem may be physiological. Professional evaluation is the logical next step.
personaContext.pack_wolf: Talk to someone you trust about what you are experiencing. Sometimes a conversation is the first step toward getting professional support.

text: You may be trading sleep for the feeling of control. Late nights often feel productive, but they quietly erode the energy that makes your daytime productive.
notes: NEW type: blind_spot. Surfaces the hidden pattern of sacrificing sleep for perceived productivity.

personaContext.watchful_deer: You feel more in control when you're working late. But the fatigue compounds — and within a few days, the thing that felt like control starts feeling like survival.
personaContext.steadfast_bear: You see late hours as discipline. But when sleep-deprived performance drops 20-30%, those extra hours aren't adding value — they're redistributing it from tomorrow.

text: Your sleep improves most when your schedule is predictable, your environment is controlled, and your wind-down is the same every night. Variability is the enemy of your sleep.
notes: NEW type: success_condition. Describes when sleep success is most likely for structured personalities.

personaContext.steadfast_bear: You already have the discipline. The gap is usually environment and consistency, not effort. Lock in the conditions and your body does the rest.
personaContext.steady_elephant: You thrive on predictability. Apply that to sleep and the results come faster than you expect — often within 5-7 days of consistent timing.

text: Start with one protein-rich meal per day. Pick breakfast or lunch and build it around eggs, legumes, poultry, fish, tofu, or dairy.
notes: Added specific protein source examples (globally neutral). Pick one meal removes overwhelm.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: Don't try to fix every meal. One meal with protein is your only job this week. Once that's automatic, the next change will feel easy.
personaContext.curious_fox: You'll want to optimize everything at once. Resist. One protein-rich meal, repeated, teaches your body what consistent fueling feels like.
personaContext.watchful_deer: Starting small means there's almost nothing to fail at. One meal, one change. That's it.
personaContext.steadfast_bear: You already train hard. This is the minimum nutrition foundation that supports what you're doing in the gym.
personaContext.steady_elephant: Pick the same meal, same protein source, same time. Repetition makes this effortless within a week.
personaContext.pack_wolf: If you eat with others, choose the protein option from whatever's available. You don't need a separate meal — just a better choice within the shared one.

text: Build each meal by putting protein on the plate first, then adding vegetables, then whatever carb or fat source is available.
notes: Rewritten from textbook-style. Now uses plate-building sequence.

personaContext.steadfast_bear: This gives you a simple decision rule for every meal. Protein first, vegetables second, then fill in the rest. No counting, no tracking.
personaContext.steady_elephant: You like systems. This is a system: a stacking order for your plate that works at home, at a restaurant, or at a buffet.

text: Do not try to fix breakfast, lunch, dinner, and snacks all at once. Change one meal at a time.
notes: Unchanged in meaning but clarified the scope (breakfast/lunch/dinner/snacks).

personaContext.shielded_turtle: Your past attempts probably failed because you tried to change everything at once. This time, change one meal. When it feels easy, add the next.
personaContext.watchful_deer: Overwhelm is what makes you quit, not lack of knowledge. One meal at a time keeps the cognitive load manageable.

text: Get your protein from food first. Supplements are useful shortcuts, not replacements for meals.
notes: Rewritten to be globally relevant. Removed supplement-first framing.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: Start with foods you already eat and identify which ones have protein. No new recipes needed — just awareness of what you already have.
personaContext.curious_fox: Explore different protein sources — there are dozens beyond chicken and eggs. Variety keeps your nutrition interesting without relying on supplements.
personaContext.watchful_deer: Read labels and compare protein content across your usual foods. The data will show you where your gaps are without needing a supplement.
personaContext.steadfast_bear: Build your meal plan around whole food protein sources. Supplements are for convenience, not the foundation of your nutrition.
personaContext.steady_elephant: Pick 4-5 reliable protein sources you like and rotate them. No need to overthink — consistency with real food outperforms any supplement strategy.
personaContext.pack_wolf: Share meals with others that naturally include protein — cooking together or eating out at places with good protein options.

text: When eating out, scan for dishes built around protein (grilled meat, fish, eggs, legumes, tofu) rather than defaulting to fried or carb-heavy options.
notes: Rewritten with specific protein examples and contrast (vs fried/carb-heavy).

personaContext.shielded_turtle: Having a simple scanning rule removes the stress of choosing. Look for protein first, then fill the rest of the plate around it.
personaContext.curious_fox: Use eating out as a chance to try new protein sources you would not cook at home — different cuisines often have great options.
personaContext.watchful_deer: Before ordering, scan the menu for protein-first dishes. Having a decision rule reduces the anxiety of choosing under social pressure.
personaContext.steadfast_bear: Apply the same protein-first rule you use at home. The setting changes but the principle does not.
personaContext.steady_elephant: Pick 2-3 go-to orders at your regular restaurants. Once you know what works, ordering becomes automatic.
personaContext.pack_wolf: When eating with others, scanning for protein-first dishes works without drawing attention to your choices. No need to announce your strategy.

text: Anchor your water intake to existing habits: a glass after waking, one before each meal, and a bottle at your desk or workspace.
notes: Rewritten with specific anchor points.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: You don't need to count ounces. Just link water to things you already do: wake up, eat, sit down to work. The anchors do the remembering for you.
personaContext.steady_elephant: Habit stacking is your strength. Water after waking, water before meals — it becomes invisible within days.

text: Do not rely on sugary drinks or energy drinks for daily energy. They spike and crash, making fatigue worse over time.
notes: Added spike-crash explanation.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: Replace one sugary drink per day with water or herbal tea. Small swaps are easier to sustain than eliminating everything at once.
personaContext.curious_fox: Try different alternatives — sparkling water, herbal teas, flavored water — until you find substitutes you actually enjoy.
personaContext.watchful_deer: Track how you feel after sugary drinks versus water over a few days. The energy crash pattern will become obvious from the data.
personaContext.steadfast_bear: Cut them out and replace with water. You do not need a gradual approach — just a clear rule.
personaContext.steady_elephant: Replace your default drink with water or a healthier option and make it the new standard. Once the habit sets, you will not miss the old one.
personaContext.pack_wolf: In social situations where others are drinking energy drinks or sugary beverages, having your own alternative ready prevents defaulting to what everyone else is having.

text: If long gaps between meals trigger cravings or overeating, eat at roughly the same times each day.
notes: Added conditional framing (if gaps trigger cravings).

personaContext.watchful_deer: When you skip meals, your body overcompensates later. Regular timing removes the decision of 'should I eat now?' and reduces the chance of reactive overeating.
personaContext.steadfast_bear: You're disciplined enough to skip meals and call it toughness. But the rebound eating later usually wipes out whatever you saved.

text: Do not skip meals to punish yourself for what you ate earlier. The restrict-binge cycle is harder to break than the original choice.
notes: Added 'punish yourself' framing and restrict-binge cycle explanation.

personaContext.watchful_deer: After eating something unplanned, the urge to skip the next meal feels logical. But restriction after indulgence creates the exact cycle that destroys consistency: eat too much, restrict, get hungry, eat too much again.

text: During busy or stressful weeks, rotate 2-3 simple meals you already know how to make instead of deciding each day.
notes: Added '2-3 meals you already know' for specificity. Reduces decision fatigue.

personaContext.watchful_deer: Decision fatigue is real. When you're stressed, the question 'what should I eat?' becomes one more problem to solve. Rotating known meals removes the question entirely.
personaContext.steady_elephant: You probably already have meals you default to. Formalize that: pick 2-3, rotate them during busy weeks, and free up mental energy for other decisions.

text: Always have one backup meal ready for days when cooking feels impossible — something you can eat in 5 minutes with zero preparation.
notes: Added '5 minutes with zero preparation' for clarity. This is the safety net.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: This is your safety net. When your energy is at zero, the backup meal prevents the default (ordering junk food or skipping eating). Stock it on weekends when your energy is higher.
personaContext.watchful_deer: On your worst days, the backup meal is the difference between eating something reasonable and eating something you'll feel bad about later. Make it effortless.

text: Pick one default breakfast and eat it every day this week. Do not think about lunch or dinner yet.
notes: Added explicit instruction to ignore other meals. Reduces scope anxiety.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: The most important thing is that you eat something reasonable in the morning without thinking about it. Same thing, every day, no decisions.
personaContext.steady_elephant: You'll appreciate the simplicity. One breakfast, locked in. It frees mental space for everything else.

text: Put nutritious foods where you can see them and reach them easily. Move less helpful options out of immediate sight.
notes: Rewritten more specifically. Visibility drives default choices.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: You eat what's easy to reach. Make the better options the easy ones and the worse options require a step. The environment does the work so you don't have to.
personaContext.steady_elephant: Organize your kitchen like you'd organize a workspace: the things you need most should be the most accessible.

text: During stressful weeks, do not keep only high-calorie trigger snacks within reach. Stock at least one alternative you feel good about.
notes: Rewritten to focus on having an alternative, not removing all treats.

personaContext.watchful_deer: When stress is high, you'll reach for whatever is closest. The goal isn't to eliminate treats — it's to make sure they're not the only option when your willpower is depleted.

text: Keep planned, nutritious snacks at your desk or workspace for stressful days so the decision is already made.
notes: Added 'so the decision is already made' — the key behavioral insight.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: When you're stressed and hungry, you'll eat the first thing you see. If that first thing is something you planned, you win without willpower.
personaContext.watchful_deer: Stress eating isn't a character flaw — it's your brain looking for fast relief. Pre-planned snacks intercept that urge with a reasonable option instead of a regrettable one.

text: Food is not morally good or bad. A cookie is not a sin. A salad is not a virtue. Remove the judgment and the guilt cycle breaks.
notes: Rewritten with vivid examples to make the concept tangible.

personaContext.watchful_deer: When you label food as 'good' or 'bad', eating something 'bad' triggers guilt, and guilt triggers more eating. Neutral language breaks the cycle at the source.

text: After overeating, eat your next normal meal at the normal time. No skipping, no compensating, no punishing. Just the next meal.
notes: Added emphasis on 'no punishing' and the concrete rule: just the next meal.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: The worst thing you can do after overeating is spiral. The best thing is boring: eat your next regular meal as if nothing happened. That's it.
personaContext.watchful_deer: Your instinct will be to restrict or skip. That instinct is the start of the cycle, not the solution. One overeating episode changes nothing if the next meal is normal.

text: At social events, eat one plate mindfully. Choose what you actually want rather than trying to eat perfectly or avoiding everything.
notes: Rewritten from vague 'use flexible eating rules'. Now has a concrete strategy.

personaContext.steadfast_bear: Rigidity at social events creates friction. One plate, eaten with attention, keeps you on track without making you the person who can't enjoy a gathering.
personaContext.pack_wolf: Social meals are part of your life. Treating them as exceptions that need to be controlled creates unnecessary stress. Enjoy them, then resume your normal pattern.

text: Do not treat one indulgent meal as a reason to abandon the rest of the day or week. One meal is one meal.
notes: Added 'one meal is one meal' as the core reframe.

personaContext.watchful_deer: The 'I already ruined today' feeling is the most dangerous moment for your consistency. It turns one unplanned meal into an unplanned day, then week. Stop the spread at one meal.
personaContext.pack_wolf: Social eating is normal. The mistake isn't the indulgent meal — it's deciding that because you had one, the rest of the day doesn't count.

text: Do not crash diet or aggressively cut calories. Extreme restriction is hard to sustain and usually leads to rebound eating.
notes: Added rebound eating consequence.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: Extreme diets feel like commitment. They're actually the fastest way to quit. A moderate approach feels less dramatic but lasts 10x longer.
personaContext.watchful_deer: Aggressive restriction creates a cycle: deprive yourself, feel deprived, overeat, feel guilty, restrict harder. A moderate deficit is boring but sustainable.

text: For fat loss, eat slightly less than usual — not dramatically less. A small deficit you barely notice is more effective long-term than a large one that breaks within weeks.
notes: Rewritten with practical guidance. 'Slightly less than usual' is clearer than 'moderate calorie reduction'.

personaContext.steadfast_bear: You have the discipline for a large deficit, but that's not the point. A deficit you can maintain for 3 months beats one you can only maintain for 3 weeks.
personaContext.steady_elephant: Small reductions compound. Cut one unnecessary portion per day and you'll see results in 4-6 weeks without ever feeling deprived.

text: Ignore detoxes, cleanses, and quick-fix nutrition claims. If it promises dramatic results in days, it does not work.
notes: Sharpened with 'if it promises dramatic results in days' test.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: These products prey on the desire for a simple fix. Real nutrition improvement is gradual and undramatic — which is exactly why it works.
personaContext.curious_fox: Your curiosity may make you want to try every new thing. Apply this filter: if it promises fast dramatic results, it is marketing, not science.
personaContext.watchful_deer: Research the claims before buying. You will find that the evidence does not support any detox or cleanse product. Trust the data.
personaContext.steadfast_bear: You are disciplined enough to follow a real nutrition plan. You do not need shortcuts — and these products are not shortcuts, they are dead ends.
personaContext.steady_elephant: Stick with the fundamentals you already know work. No product will outperform consistent, balanced eating over time.
personaContext.pack_wolf: When friends or social media promote these products, having a clear mental filter saves you from social pressure purchases.

text: Do not isolate yourself socially because of diet rules. If your nutrition plan requires you to avoid friends and family meals, the plan is the problem.
notes: Added 'the plan is the problem' — shifts blame from user to plan.

personaContext.watchful_deer: You may withdraw socially to avoid food-related anxiety. But isolation increases stress, which undermines your nutrition goals. Stay connected — adjust portions, not attendance.
personaContext.pack_wolf: Your social life is a source of energy and accountability. A nutrition approach that cuts you off from it is working against your strongest asset.

text: During travel, keep one nutrition anchor: one protein-rich meal per day, no matter what else happens.
notes: Unchanged in concept, clarified the anchor (one protein meal).

personaContext.shielded_turtle: Travel makes everything unpredictable. One anchor keeps the thread alive without requiring you to replicate your home routine in a hotel.
personaContext.steadfast_bear: You want to maintain standards while traveling. One protein meal is the minimum viable standard — protect it and let everything else flex.

text: When you don't control the menu (cafeteria, family meals, catered events), use this rule: pick the highest-protein option, add vegetables if available, eat until satisfied.
notes: Rewritten with concrete 3-step decision rule.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: When you cannot control the menu, the three-step rule removes decision pressure. Highest protein, add vegetables, moderate the rest. Simple and repeatable.
personaContext.steady_elephant: Three-step rules work for you. Highest protein, add vegetables, eat until satisfied. No thinking required.

text: If you train hard, do not chronically under-eat. Under-fueling impairs recovery, worsens mood, and eventually kills consistency.
notes: Added consequences (mood, consistency) to make the impact tangible.

personaContext.steadfast_bear: You might equate eating less with being disciplined. But if you train hard and eat too little, your body starts borrowing from recovery, mood, and energy. The debt shows up in performance.

text: Food is fuel and recovery, not a reward or punishment. When you eat well, it is not because you earned it. When you eat poorly, it is not because you failed.
notes: Rewritten with reward/punishment framing to make it tangible.

personaContext.watchful_deer: The reward/punishment framing is what creates guilt spirals. Remove the moral weight from food and the emotional eating cycle loses its engine.
personaContext.steadfast_bear: You earn your food by being alive, not by training. That shift in framing makes nutrition sustainable instead of transactional.

text: A boring, consistent diet you follow for 6 months will always outperform an exciting, intense diet you follow for 3 weeks.
notes: Rewritten with time comparison to make the point vivid. Downgraded to supporting.

personaContext.steadfast_bear: You're drawn to intense approaches because they feel like real commitment. But the approach that works is the one you're still following in 6 months, not the one that felt most disciplined in week 1.
personaContext.steady_elephant: Consistency is your advantage. A simple nutritional approach that you execute reliably will beat any complex plan.

text: If you have persistent digestive issues, medically restricted diets, or are pregnant/postpartum, get professional nutritional guidance before making major changes.
notes: Merged NUT029 and NUT030 into one safety recommendation.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: Seeking professional nutrition advice is not overreacting — it is the responsible step when you have specific health conditions.
personaContext.curious_fox: Do not try to self-manage complex dietary needs through experimentation. A professional can give you a framework that is safe to explore within.
personaContext.watchful_deer: Your tendency to research is valuable, but medical nutrition needs professional oversight. Use your research to ask better questions, not to self-prescribe.
personaContext.steadfast_bear: Discipline alone cannot solve medical nutrition needs. A professional gives you the right plan to be disciplined about.
personaContext.steady_elephant: Get professional guidance and then build your consistent routine around it. The right system matters more than rigid adherence to the wrong one.
personaContext.pack_wolf: Ask people you trust for referrals to nutrition professionals. Personal recommendations often lead to better provider relationships.

text: Use meal tracking only if it gives you clarity without stress. If logging food makes you anxious or obsessive, stop and eat by feel instead.
notes: Renumbered from NUT031. Added explicit 'eat by feel' alternative.

personaContext.curious_fox: You might enjoy tracking because it satisfies your curiosity. That's fine — but if it shifts from insight to obsession, it's working against you.
personaContext.steady_elephant: Tracking can be a useful tool for your systematic approach. Just monitor whether it's adding clarity or adding stress.

text: Try one new healthy recipe per week if variety keeps you engaged. If repetition works better for you, ignore this entirely.
notes: Added explicit permission to ignore if repetition works better.

personaContext.curious_fox: Your need for novelty is real. One new recipe per week channels that curiosity without destabilizing your base meals. The key is adding variety on top of consistency, not instead of it.

text: You may be using food to manage emotions you have not named yet. Stress eating is rarely about hunger — it is about soothing something that food cannot fix.
notes: NEW type: blind_spot. Surfaces the hidden emotional driver behind stress eating.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: You might not realize how often you eat to fill a gap that isn't hunger. The first step is noticing: ask yourself 'am I hungry or am I uncomfortable?' before reaching for food.
personaContext.watchful_deer: Your emotional sensitivity means you feel stress physically. Food is a fast, available comfort. Naming the emotion — tired, anxious, overwhelmed — often reduces the craving more than willpower does.

text: Your nutrition stays on track when meals are planned in advance, options are limited (not overwhelming), and the food environment supports your goals. Decision fatigue is your biggest nutritional enemy.
notes: NEW type: success_condition. Describes when nutrition consistency is most likely.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: You succeed when everything is pre-decided. The fewer choices you have to make about food each day, the better your nutrition will be. Remove the decisions and the results follow.
personaContext.steady_elephant: Your strength is systems. Apply it to nutrition: a weekly meal plan, a shopping list, and a backup meal. When the system runs, you don't need motivation.

text: Start with a 10-20 minute session of something you can do without instruction: a brisk walk, bodyweight squats, or a follow-along video. The goal this week is showing up, not optimizing.
notes: Added concrete examples and explicit 'showing up not optimizing' framing.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: You don't need a perfect plan. A 10-minute walk counts. Starting imperfectly is infinitely better than waiting for the right moment.
personaContext.watchful_deer: The pressure to do it right can stop you from doing it at all. Any movement this week is a win. Optimization comes later.

text: Aim to show up 3-4 times per week at any effort level before worrying about how hard you train.
notes: Rewritten from abstract 'focus on consistency before intensity'. Downgraded to supporting. Added specific frequency.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: The hardest part for you is showing up, not the workout itself. Count appearances, not intensity. Three easy sessions beats zero perfect ones.
personaContext.curious_fox: Frequency first, optimization later. You can explore different workout styles while building the habit of consistent attendance.
personaContext.watchful_deer: Do not wait until you have the perfect plan. Show up three times this week doing anything — walk, stretch, bodyweight. Refine later.
personaContext.steadfast_bear: You already know how to show up. The reminder here is: easy weeks count. A light session still maintains the frequency habit.
personaContext.steady_elephant: Build the schedule first, then optimize what you do in each session. Your strength is in routine — use it to lock in the frequency.
personaContext.pack_wolf: Coordinate with a partner or group for 3-4 sessions per week. The social commitment makes the frequency automatic.

text: Do not attempt advanced exercises before you are comfortable with basic movement patterns. Complexity without confidence causes injury and discouragement.
notes: Added consequence (injury and discouragement) to make the warning tangible.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: Advanced exercises look impressive but they're built on basics. Master the simple movements first and the advanced ones will come naturally.
personaContext.watchful_deer: Struggling with an exercise that's too hard can feel like proof that you don't belong at the gym. It's not — it just means the exercise was too advanced for now.

text: Walking is a real workout. A daily 20-minute walk improves energy, mood, and cardiovascular health more than any plan you do not follow.
notes: Rewritten to remove condescending 'legitimate'. Added comparison to unfollowed plans.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: If the gym feels like too much, walking is not a compromise — it's a starting point that works. Many people maintain excellent health with walking alone.
personaContext.steadfast_bear: You might dismiss walking as not 'real' exercise. But if you're currently sedentary, walking produces measurable improvements in heart health and energy within weeks.

text: Today, take a 10-minute walk after any meal. Do not plan it — just do it once and notice how you feel afterward.
notes: Changed 'one meal' to 'any meal' and added 'notice how you feel' for self-awareness.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: One walk. No preparation. No equipment. No plan beyond 'walk for 10 minutes after eating.' This is the lowest-barrier action you can take.
personaContext.curious_fox: Try different routes or times of day. The novelty of a new path can make a simple walk feel like a mini-exploration.
personaContext.watchful_deer: Do not overthink this. No specific pace, no step target, no tracking required. Just walk after a meal and notice how your body feels.
personaContext.steadfast_bear: This is beneath your level — and that is the point. It builds a daily movement anchor that survives your hardest weeks.
personaContext.steady_elephant: Attach it to the same meal every day. After lunch, walk. Once it is part of your routine, it will run on autopilot.
personaContext.pack_wolf: Walk with someone — a colleague after lunch, a family member after dinner. The conversation makes the walk disappear.

text: Set one specific time during desk-heavy workdays to stand up and move for 5-10 minutes. Anchor it to lunch, a meeting end, or a recurring alarm.
notes: Added specific anchor points (lunch, meeting end, alarm) and duration.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: Set a quiet alarm. When it goes off, stand up and stretch for 5 minutes. No one needs to notice. It is just you taking a break.
personaContext.steady_elephant: Anchor this to something you already do. After lunch or after a recurring meeting — the trigger makes it automatic.

text: Start strength training with 4-5 basic exercises: squat, push-up, row, plank, and a hip hinge. Master these before adding complexity.
notes: Rewritten with 5 specific exercises instead of vague 'simple movement patterns'.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: Five exercises is all you need to start. You can learn them from a single video or one session with a trainer. Don't let complexity delay your start.
personaContext.steady_elephant: A clear, finite list works for you. Five exercises, repeated 2-3 times per week, with small progressions. Simple system, clear results.

text: Once you are showing up consistently, add strength training 2-3 times per week. Consistency first, then structure.
notes: Added prerequisite (showing up consistently) as a gate.

personaContext.steadfast_bear: You probably want to jump straight to a structured program. That's fine — but only if you've been showing up consistently for 2-3 weeks first.
personaContext.steady_elephant: 2-3 times per week, same days, same time. The frequency is more important than the complexity.

text: Add a small amount of weight, one extra rep, or one more set every 1-2 weeks. Progress should feel gradual, not dramatic.
notes: Rewritten from jargon 'progressive overload' to concrete actions.

personaContext.steadfast_bear: Small increments feel underwhelming but they compound. Adding 2.5kg per month means 30kg in a year. That's how real strength is built.
personaContext.steady_elephant: Track your progressions. A simple log showing weights and reps gives you objective proof that the system is working.

text: Do not dramatically increase training volume from one week to the next. Large jumps increase injury risk and fatigue.
notes: Added consequences (injury risk and fatigue).

personaContext.steadfast_bear: Your instinct after a good week is to add more. But your body adapts to the current load before it can handle the next. Patience here prevents forced rest later.
personaContext.steady_elephant: Your consistency means you can sustain gradual increases better than most. Trust your steady pace — small weekly increments compound into significant progress over months.

text: Your identity should be 'someone who returns quickly' not 'someone who never misses.' Perfection breaks. Return speed is the real skill.
notes: Merged FIT011 and FIT028. Stronger phrasing with identity framing.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: When you miss a session, the gap between missing and returning is where most progress is lost. Your only job after a miss is to shorten that gap.
personaContext.watchful_deer: You feel missed workouts emotionally. The shame makes the gap longer. Reframe: the miss was a pause, not a failure. The next session is session #2, not a restart.

text: After a missed workout, do the smallest possible version next time: a 10-minute walk, 5 push-ups, one set of anything. The goal is to show up, not to compensate.
notes: Added concrete micro-examples (10-min walk, 5 push-ups, one set).

personaContext.shielded_turtle: The smaller the comeback session, the less activation energy it requires. 5 push-ups is enough to prove you're still in the game.
personaContext.watchful_deer: You don't need to make up for the miss. You just need to show up. Even the tiniest session resets the psychological counter from 'I stopped' to 'I'm still going.'

text: Prepare your workout clothes, shoes, and equipment the night before. Remove the setup friction so the only decision left is to start.
notes: Added 'the night before' and 'the only decision left is to start'.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: Every small friction point is an excuse for your brain to say 'not today.' Removing them the night before means tomorrow-morning-you has one fewer barrier.
personaContext.steady_elephant: Preparation is something you naturally do well. Apply it here: clothes out, shoes ready, water bottle filled. Morning-you just has to move.

text: Add small variety within your plan (different exercises for the same muscle group, a new warm-up) without replacing the entire plan.
notes: Added specific examples of controlled variety.

personaContext.curious_fox: Your need for novelty is real — channel it into the details (new exercises, new music, new route) while keeping the structure intact. Variety within consistency.

text: Do not replace your entire workout plan every week. Progress requires repeating the same exercises long enough to improve at them.
notes: Downgraded from core to supporting. Added explanation of why repetition matters.

personaContext.curious_fox: Switching plans feels like progress because it's new and exciting. But the excitement of starting is not the same as the progress of continuing. Stick with a plan for at least 4-6 weeks.

text: Do not default to high-intensity training during weeks of poor sleep or high stress. Match your training intensity to your recovery capacity.
notes: Added 'match intensity to recovery capacity' as the guiding principle.

personaContext.watchful_deer: When you're already depleted, a hard session doesn't build you up — it digs a deeper hole. A moderate session on a bad day protects the rest of your week.
personaContext.steadfast_bear: Pushing through poor recovery feels disciplined but it reduces your capacity for the sessions that actually matter. Train at 60% when recovery is low and save 100% for when your body is ready.

text: Do not make every workout a maximal effort session. Sustainable training alternates between hard, moderate, and easy days.
notes: Added the alternation principle (hard, moderate, easy).

personaContext.steadfast_bear: Going all-out every session feels productive but it's actually the fastest route to burnout. The best training programs have easy days by design, not by accident.
personaContext.steady_elephant: Structure your week with intentional intensity variation. Not every session needs to be hard — that's not laziness, it's programming.

text: If the gym feels intimidating, start at home, outdoors, or in a quiet environment until your confidence builds. The setting should support your return, not block it.
notes: Merged FIT019 and FIT020. Added 'the setting should support your return, not block it.'

personaContext.shielded_turtle: You don't owe the gym anything. If home workouts get you moving, that's where you start. The gym is an option for later, not a requirement for now.
personaContext.watchful_deer: If crowded or competitive environments drain your confidence, avoiding them early is smart, not weak. Build your baseline in a space that feels safe, then expand when you're ready.

text: If social energy helps you stay consistent, use a workout partner, group class, or fitness community. If it creates pressure, train alone.
notes: Merged FIT021 and FIT022. Added the explicit 'if it creates pressure, train alone' escape.

personaContext.pack_wolf: Social accountability is your superpower. But watch for the line between 'this is fun' and 'I'm comparing myself to everyone.' If comparison creeps in, the social element is working against you.

text: During travel or disrupted weeks, switch to bodyweight workouts, hotel room exercises, or walking. Protect the habit, not the intensity.
notes: Merged FIT023 and FIT024. 'Protect the habit, not the intensity' is the key insight.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: Travel will never match your home routine. Don't try. A 15-minute hotel room session keeps the habit alive until you're back to normal.
personaContext.steadfast_bear: You'll be tempted to skip entirely because you can't do your full workout. A bodyweight session in a hotel room keeps the pattern alive. The habit matters more than the load.

text: If sitting all day makes your back stiff or hips tight, 5-10 minutes of stretching after your workout or as a standalone session can noticeably improve how you feel by evening.
notes: Rewritten from vague 'use mobility work to support comfort'. Now addresses specific pain points.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: Stretching at home is low-pressure and requires no equipment. Start with whatever feels tight after sitting — even 5 minutes makes a difference.
personaContext.steadfast_bear: Mobility work is not optional recovery — it is injury prevention that protects your ability to train hard. Five minutes of stretching preserves months of training.

text: Do not push through sharp, worsening, or persistent pain. Pain that gets worse during exercise is a signal to stop and modify, not to push harder.
notes: Added 'signal to stop and modify' to give a clear action.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: Pain can feel confusing and scary. The rule is simple: if it gets worse during exercise, stop. You are not being weak — you are being smart.
personaContext.curious_fox: Your tendency to push through and try something new instead of resting can worsen injuries. Pain that increases during movement is not a challenge to overcome.
personaContext.watchful_deer: You may analyze whether the pain is 'real enough' to stop. If it worsens during exercise, that is your answer. Stop and get it assessed.
personaContext.steadfast_bear: You're more likely to push through pain because you associate stopping with weakness. Sharp or worsening pain is your body protecting itself, not being soft.
personaContext.steady_elephant: Skipping a workout for pain feels like breaking your routine. But training through worsening pain breaks your routine for much longer.
personaContext.pack_wolf: Do not push through pain to keep up with your group. Your body's signals override the group's pace. Step back, recover, and return fully.

text: If you have persistent pain, a current injury, or doctor-imposed restrictions, get professional guidance before starting or modifying any exercise program.
notes: Safety escalation. Unchanged in intent.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: Getting professional clearance before starting is not overcaution — it is the right first step when you have existing issues.
personaContext.curious_fox: Do not experiment with injury recovery. A professional gives you the safe boundaries within which you can explore movement.
personaContext.watchful_deer: Your research is valuable for asking informed questions, but injury management needs professional assessment, not self-diagnosis.
personaContext.steadfast_bear: Working through restrictions without guidance is not toughness — it is a risk that can sideline you far longer than the original issue.
personaContext.steady_elephant: A professional gives you a clear recovery plan you can follow consistently. That structure is more effective than guessing.
personaContext.pack_wolf: Ask training partners or gym staff for referrals to physiotherapists or sports medicine professionals. Personal recommendations often lead to better care.

text: Do not choose workouts only because they burn the most calories. A workout you enjoy and repeat beats a workout you dread and skip.
notes: Added the contrast: enjoy and repeat vs dread and skip.

personaContext.watchful_deer: If you hate the workout, you'll find reasons to skip it. Enjoyment isn't a luxury — it's what makes the habit last.
personaContext.steadfast_bear: Calorie burn is not the only measure of a productive workout. Strength, mobility, and consistency matter more than the number on the screen.

text: Open your calendar right now and block a specific time slot for your next workout. A scheduled session is twice as likely to happen as an unscheduled one.
notes: Added 'right now' urgency and the 2x statistic for motivation.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: Don't leave it vague. Pick a day, pick a time, put it in your calendar. Scheduled workouts get done. Planned-in-your-head workouts usually don't.
personaContext.steady_elephant: You respond well to structure. A calendar block turns a wish into a commitment.

text: Keep a shorter backup workout (10-15 minutes) for days when your full session is not possible. Showing up briefly is always better than skipping entirely.
notes: NEW. Addresses the all-or-nothing pattern identified in review.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: The all-or-nothing trap: if you can't do 45 minutes, you skip entirely. A 10-minute backup workout breaks that pattern. Something always beats nothing.
personaContext.watchful_deer: When you skip because you can't do the full workout, the skip feels like failure. A short backup means you never have to skip — you just scale down.
personaContext.steady_elephant: Have two versions of your workout: the full version and the minimum viable version. Both count.

text: Use a wearable, step counter, or simple log only if it motivates you. If it becomes a source of pressure or guilt, remove it.
notes: Renumbered from FIT031.

personaContext.curious_fox: You might enjoy the data. That's fine — but if you find yourself feeling bad about 'red' days or unmet goals, the tracker is hurting more than helping.
personaContext.steady_elephant: A simple tracking system complements your structured approach. Just make sure it's serving you, not stressing you.

text: You may be caught in the all-or-nothing trap: if you cannot do the perfect workout, you skip entirely. Over time, the skipped days cost you far more than imperfect sessions would.
notes: NEW type: blind_spot. Surfaces the all-or-nothing pattern that silently kills consistency.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: You wait for the right conditions: enough time, enough energy, the right plan. But those conditions rarely arrive. The imperfect session you do is worth more than the perfect session you skip.
personaContext.watchful_deer: The perfectionism isn't about the workout — it's about avoiding the feeling of doing something imperfectly. But skipping feels worse in the long run.
personaContext.steadfast_bear: Your standards are high. That's a strength — until it means you'd rather do nothing than do less than your best. The discipline of scaling down is harder than the discipline of going hard.

text: You stay active most consistently when the barrier to starting is near zero, the session length is realistic for your schedule, and the activity does not depend on willpower alone. Design your system for low-friction execution.
notes: NEW type: success_condition. Describes when fitness consistency is most likely for low-activation profiles.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: You succeed when the setup is already done, the session is short, and the bar for 'good enough' is low. That's not lazy — it's smart system design for your personality.
personaContext.watchful_deer: You succeed when the environment feels safe, the expectations are modest, and no one is watching or judging. Build your early fitness habits in those conditions.

text: You do not have to feel motivated, energized, or ready to act. Show up on bad days too. The habit survives because of the days you did not feel like it.
notes: Rewritten from abstract 'consistency over emotional perfection'. Downgraded to supporting.

personaContext.watchful_deer: You wait for the 'right' emotional state to act. But motivation follows action, not the other way around. Start even when you feel flat.
personaContext.steadfast_bear: You already understand discipline. This is the reminder: discipline includes the days when it doesn't feel disciplined, just mechanical.

text: Pick one small, measurable win to achieve each day — one meal, one walk, one stretch, one glass of water. Stack confidence from the smallest proof.
notes: Added concrete examples and 'stack confidence from smallest proof'.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: Confidence doesn't come from big achievements. It comes from repeated small proofs that you can follow through. One win per day compounds faster than you expect.
personaContext.watchful_deer: When confidence is low, the smallest evidence of success matters most. One completed task proves 'I can do this' in a way that planning never does.

text: Do not use shame, guilt, or harsh self-criticism as motivation. It creates avoidance, not action.
notes: Merged MW003 and MW016 into one clear statement. Added 'creates avoidance, not action.'

personaContext.shielded_turtle: The voice that says 'you should be ashamed' after a miss doesn't motivate you — it makes you avoid the thing you missed. Replace shame with curiosity: 'what got in the way?'
personaContext.watchful_deer: You already feel setbacks deeply. Adding self-criticism on top doesn't create motivation — it creates a cycle of shame and avoidance that makes the next attempt harder.

text: After your last work task, spend 10-15 minutes on something non-productive: a walk, music, sitting quietly, a shower. This separates your performance self from your resting self.
notes: Rewritten from vague 'build emotional decompression'. Now has specific duration, examples, and rationale.

personaContext.watchful_deer: Without a buffer between work and evening, stress carries into everything: your sleep, your food choices, your relationships. The buffer doesn't fix the stress — it contains it.
personaContext.steadfast_bear: You push through without stopping. But 15 minutes of deliberate non-productivity makes the remaining evening more restorative than powering through without a break.

text: When stress spikes, try one of these: 5 slow breaths (in for 4, out for 6), step outside for 2 minutes with no phone, or close your eyes and count backward from 10. Pick whichever you will actually do.
notes: Rewritten with 3 concrete options. Added 'pick whichever you will actually do'.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: When you're already drained, you need a reset that requires zero activation energy. Don't pick the best option — pick the easiest one. The bar is 'will I actually do this when I'm overwhelmed.'
personaContext.watchful_deer: These work not because they're magical but because they interrupt the stress loop. Your brain needs a few seconds of non-stress input to reset. Any of these provides that.

text: Set a time after which you do not check work messages. Tell people your cutoff so they stop expecting replies.
notes: Rewritten from vague 'create healthy boundaries around work spillover'.

personaContext.watchful_deer: Every notification after hours reactivates your stress response. A communicated cutoff isn't rude — it's a boundary that protects your recovery.
personaContext.steady_elephant: Make it a rule. Rules are your strength. 'No work messages after 8 PM' is clean, clear, and enforceable.

text: When you miss a workout or eat off-plan, say to yourself: 'That happened. My next meal or session is where I pick up.' Do not say: 'I ruined everything.'
notes: Rewritten with exact script for what to say vs what not to say.

personaContext.watchful_deer: The language you use with yourself after a setback determines how quickly you return. 'I ruined everything' extends the gap. 'That happened, here's my next step' closes it.

text: When you feel overwhelmed, plant your feet, notice 3 things you can see, and take 5 slow breaths. This interrupts the stress loop and gives your brain a few seconds to reset.
notes: Rewritten from jargon 'use grounding or breathing'. Now is a specific technique.

personaContext.watchful_deer: This works because it forces your brain to switch from internal spiraling to external observation. 3 things you can see pulls your attention out of the worry loop.

text: Do not turn a temporary setback into a permanent conclusion. One bad day does not mean you are bad at this.
notes: Merged MW009 and SLP024. One version for the whole system.

personaContext.watchful_deer: Your brain generalizes from single events. One missed day becomes 'I always fail.' That's not a fact — it's an emotional interpretation. Challenge it.

text: Do not treat a missed habit as proof that you are undisciplined. Missed days are data, not verdicts.
notes: Added 'data, not verdicts' framing.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: A missed day tells you something (too tired? too complex? bad timing?) — it doesn't define you. Use it as information, not as evidence of failure.
personaContext.watchful_deer: You tend to make missed days mean something about your character. They don't. They mean something about the conditions. Change the conditions, not the self-assessment.

text: When stress is high, shrink the habit to its smallest version instead of dropping it entirely. 5 minutes of movement beats 0 minutes.
notes: Excellent recommendation. Added specific example (5 min vs 0 min).

personaContext.shielded_turtle: Your instinct under stress is to drop everything. Instead, make the habit so small it requires almost no energy. That keeps the thread alive until the stress passes.
personaContext.watchful_deer: Dropping the habit entirely means restarting later, which is much harder. A tiny version preserves the identity of 'I'm still doing this' even on terrible days.

text: Do not wait to feel motivated before taking action. Motivation follows action, not the other way around. Start, and the feeling catches up.
notes: Added 'motivation follows action' insight. This is the key behavioral flip.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: You're waiting for the feeling of readiness. It usually doesn't arrive on its own. But if you start — even reluctantly — the motivation often appears within 5 minutes.
personaContext.watchful_deer: The trap is believing you need to feel a certain way before you can act. You don't. Some of your best sessions will start when you felt the least like doing them.

text: Small actions count when they keep the system alive. A 5-minute walk, one healthy meal, or going to bed on time — these are not trivial when they maintain momentum.
notes: Merged MW014 and MW015. Added specific examples and 'maintain momentum' framing.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: You might dismiss small actions because they don't feel like 'real' progress. They are. They're the thread that connects your good weeks. Without them, every restart costs more.

text: Pick one thing you can repeat this week — a walk, a meal, a bedtime. Doing it three times will build more confidence than any comparison or plan.
notes: Rewritten from abstract 'build confidence through repeatable action'. Now has specific instruction.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: Pick the smallest thing you are willing to do three times. A 10-minute walk, a glass of water with breakfast, lights out by a set time. Three repetitions build evidence that you can follow through.
personaContext.watchful_deer: Confidence comes from evidence: 'I said I would do this, and I did.' Three repetitions of anything this week gives you that evidence.

text: Do not compare your starting point with someone else's middle. Your pace, your conditions, your baseline are different — and that is not a weakness.
notes: Rewritten from generic 'avoid comparing your pace'. Added 'starting point vs middle' contrast.

personaContext.watchful_deer: Social media and gym environments make comparison automatic. Remind yourself: you're seeing their performance, not their starting point, their setbacks, or their struggles.
personaContext.pack_wolf: You draw energy from others, but comparison is the shadow side of that. Use the group for motivation, not as a benchmark. Their journey is different from yours.

text: If metric tracking (weight, calories, steps, sleep scores) increases your anxiety, reduce or remove the tracking. Progress should be felt, not only measured.
notes: Merged MW020 and SLP025 into one universal tracking-anxiety recommendation.

personaContext.watchful_deer: You check the numbers hoping for reassurance, but a bad number triggers more anxiety than a good number provides relief. If tracking makes you feel worse, it's not serving you.

text: If planning has become a substitute for action, set a deadline: plan for 2 days maximum, then start regardless of how incomplete the plan feels.
notes: Rewritten with a concrete rule (2-day planning deadline).

personaContext.curious_fox: You love the research and planning phase because it feels productive. Set a hard limit: 2 days of planning, then start. The plan will never feel ready — that's normal.
personaContext.watchful_deer: Planning feels safe because it doesn't require vulnerability. But at some point, the plan becomes a hiding place. Two days, then go.

text: After a bad week, ask yourself one question: 'What happened right before I fell off track?' The answer is usually a stressor, not a character flaw.
notes: Rewritten from abstract 'use reflection to identify triggers'. Now has one specific question.

personaContext.curious_fox: Your analytical nature is an asset here. Use it to understand your patterns, not to judge yourself. One diagnostic question after a setback is worth more than a week of vague self-criticism.
personaContext.watchful_deer: When you fall off track, your first instinct is 'what's wrong with me?' The better question is 'what happened around me?' — the answer is usually external, not internal.

text: Self-care is not indulgence. Recovery, rest, and enjoyment are structural requirements for sustainable performance — not rewards you have to earn.
notes: Rewritten from vague 'treat self-care as support, not indulgence'.

personaContext.watchful_deer: You may feel guilty about resting because it looks like inaction. But rest is a performance input, not a reward. Without it, your careful planning cannot produce results.
personaContext.steadfast_bear: You're more likely to skip rest because it feels unproductive. Reframe: rest is maintenance, not downtime. A machine that never stops for maintenance breaks.

text: Do not glorify being exhausted. Working until you drop is not discipline — it is a system that will eventually fail.
notes: Sharpened language. Made the consequence explicit.

personaContext.steadfast_bear: The culture around you may reward exhaustion. But burnout doesn't announce itself — it arrives as declining performance, worsening mood, and loss of interest in things you used to enjoy.
personaContext.steady_elephant: Sustainability is a system property. A system that runs at 100% with no maintenance breaks will always fail eventually. Build recovery into the design.

text: During demanding work periods, schedule recovery breaks the same way you schedule meetings. If it is not in the calendar, it will not happen.
notes: Added calendar scheduling metaphor.

personaContext.steadfast_bear: You schedule training sessions and work meetings. Recovery breaks deserve the same treatment. If it is not in the calendar, your default mode will skip it.
personaContext.steady_elephant: You treat calendar items as commitments. Put recovery breaks on the calendar and treat them with the same respect you give meetings.

text: If having someone to answer to improves your consistency, use gentle accountability: a friend who checks in, a shared goal, or a coach. Not someone who shames you.
notes: Added 'not someone who shames you' as the critical distinction.

personaContext.watchful_deer: Choose someone who checks in without judging. The right accountability partner asks 'how did it go?' not 'why didn't you do it?' Supportive, not evaluative.
personaContext.pack_wolf: Accountability works for you — but only when it's supportive. If the person checking in makes you feel guilty instead of supported, the accountability is toxic, not helpful.

text: Do not announce your goals publicly too early if the pressure of others watching creates more anxiety than motivation.
notes: Rewritten to target premature public commitment specifically.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: Public goals create external pressure. If that pressure motivates you, great. If it makes you avoid the goal because now people are watching, keep it private until you have momentum.
personaContext.watchful_deer: The fear of public failure can be worse than the motivation of public accountability. Earn your own confidence first, then share if and when it helps.

text: Use mindfulness or journaling only if it helps you process thoughts calmly. If it becomes another source of pressure or self-analysis, it is not working for you.
notes: Merged MW028 and MW031. Added explicit escape clause.

personaContext.curious_fox: You might enjoy journaling or meditation as a form of self-exploration. That's healthy — until it tips into rumination or performance anxiety about doing it 'right.'
personaContext.watchful_deer: If mindfulness makes you more aware of your anxiety rather than less, it's not the right tool for you right now. That's okay. Try something physical instead (walking, stretching).

text: During major life transitions (new job, new baby, relocation, caregiving), use flexible mini-routines instead of your normal system. Protect the thread, not the structure.
notes: Added specific transition examples and 'protect the thread, not the structure'.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: Transitions make your normal routine impossible. Don't fight that. A 5-minute mini-routine during chaos is worth more than a full routine you can't execute.
personaContext.steadfast_bear: You'll want to maintain your full system. You can't. Instead of seeing the transition as a failure of discipline, see it as a period that requires a different kind of discipline: flexibility.

text: If stress, anxiety, or emotional distress persists for more than 4 weeks and affects your daily functioning, seek professional support. This is not a wellness problem — it is a health need.
notes: Added 4-week timeline and 'affects daily functioning' threshold.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: Reaching out for help is not a sign of failure. If stress or anxiety has persisted for weeks, a professional can provide the safety and guidance you need.
personaContext.curious_fox: You may keep trying new coping strategies instead of seeking help. If 4 weeks of self-management has not worked, professional support is the next step.
personaContext.watchful_deer: You may analyze your distress instead of acting on it. After 4 weeks, the analysis is complete — the answer is professional support.
personaContext.steadfast_bear: Mental health is not something you can push through with discipline. Persistent distress requires professional expertise, not more effort.
personaContext.steady_elephant: If your usual coping routine is not working after 4 weeks, the situation is beyond routine management. A professional can help you build a new system.
personaContext.pack_wolf: Talk to someone you trust about how you are feeling. Social connection is your strength — use it as the bridge to professional help if needed.

text: You may be waiting for Monday, next month, or the 'right time' to restart. That waiting period is not neutral — it quietly erodes your confidence and makes the restart feel bigger than it actually is.
notes: NEW type: blind_spot. Surfaces the 'waiting for the right time' pattern.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: You tell yourself 'I'll start Monday' or 'after this stressful period.' But the longer you wait, the more the restart feels like a mountain. Start with something tiny today — the restart has already happened.
personaContext.watchful_deer: Every day you wait adds guilt and distance. The restart doesn't need to be dramatic. One small action today — a walk, a meal, a glass of water — is the restart.

text: Your likely wellness cycle: motivation spike, aggressive planning, disruption, guilt, extended restart delay. The cycle does not break with more discipline — it breaks with a smaller, disruption-proof plan.
notes: NEW type: pattern_prediction. Describes the common restart cycle.

personaContext.shielded_turtle: You've been through this cycle before. The fix isn't trying harder during the motivation spike — it's building a plan so simple that it survives the disruption phase. That's the only change that matters.
personaContext.curious_fox: You recognize this pattern because you're self-aware enough to see it. The trap is using that awareness to plan a better version of the same cycle. Instead, make the plan small enough that the cycle can't break it.
personaContext.watchful_deer: Each time you go through this cycle, the guilt phase gets longer and the restart delay grows. The exit isn't at the motivation end — it's at the planning end. Make the plan tiny.

text: Your emotional sensitivity is not a weakness — it means you notice patterns others miss, you respond to feedback faster, and you adjust sooner when something is not working.
notes: NEW type: strength_insight. Reframes emotional sensitivity as an asset.
